# Tech Due Diligence Report: KRC Focus Inc.
**Date:** July 19, 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 2 (April 6, 2025 - July 6, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified

## Company Overview
| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Legal Name** | KRC Focus Inc. (dba Tier One AI Labs) |
| **Product** | Tier One AI Labs Platform — AI assurance platform for advisory firms providing workflow automation, custom LLM solutions, and data integration |
| **HQ** | 447 Broadway #828, New York, NY 10023 |
| **Cloud** | Google Cloud Platform (Kubernetes Engine, Cloud Storage, Artifact Registry) accessed via Supabase carve-out |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure (5/10)
- **Architecture:** Single cloud provider (Supabase with underlying GCP services) with multi-AZ deployment and Cloud VPN
- **Positive:** Real network diagram provided (not placeholder); Kubernetes Engine indicates container orchestration maturity
- **Risks:** No named regions, no CDN, no secondary cloud provider; heavy reliance on single subservice organization creates concentration risk

### Application Architecture (4/10)
- **Deployment:** Kubernetes container orchestration confirmed by network diagram (meaningful differentiator vs. VM-based deployments)
- **Process:** Separate environments enforced; branch protection to master branch required
- **Gap:** Languages/frameworks not disclosed; architectural pattern unspecified beyond containerization

### Data Layer (5/10)
- **Storage:** Supabase (PostgreSQL) primary database with per-tenant segregation
- **Protection:** Daily backups, multi-AZ configuration, data classification and retention policies
- **Infrastructure:** Cloud Storage visible in diagram; Active Directory mentioned for infrastructure accounts
- **Gap:** No caching layer or data warehouse disclosed

### Security (6/10)
- **Access Controls:** MFA enforced for all admin/remote access; quarterly access reviews including privileged accounts
- **Monitoring:** IDS/IPS and firewalls (application gateway + production network); log protection in place
- **Testing:** Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; monthly automated vulnerability scanning
- **Governance:** Cybersecurity insurance maintained; network diagram reviewed annually by IT management
- **Gaps:** WAF not mentioned despite web-based AI platform; SIEM tool unnamed

### DevOps (6/10)
- **Pipeline:** Branch protection and mandatory code review prior to production deployment confirmed tested
- **Infrastructure:** Infrastructure-as-code with rollback capability; automatic patching with monthly ITLC review
- **Governance:** Weekly ITLC meetings for operational oversight indicate mature startup governance
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tool and source control tool not named by product

### Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery (6/10)
- **Planning:** BCDR policy documented, annually reviewed and tested across multiple loss scenarios (stronger than tabletop-only peers)
- **Technical:** Daily backups with multi-AZ failover (spinning up servers across all AZs)
- **Response:** Incident response plan tested periodically (CC7.3.3 confirmed tested)
- **Gaps:** No RTO/RPO stated; no multi-region failover capability

### Vendor Diversity (2/10)
- **Critical Dependency:** Complete reliance on single subservice organization (Supabase)
- **Structure:** GCP services (Kubernetes, Cloud Storage, Artifact Registry) accessed only via Supabase carve-out treatment
- **Risk:** No secondary cloud or CDN provider; all physical security and data center controls depend on one vendor

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Supabase** | Critical | Data center/cloud infrastructure services: database, networking, servers, VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security, multi-AZ availability | **Severe concentration risk** — single point of failure for all infrastructure controls; carve-out treatment means reliance on Supabase's SOC 2 without direct GCP contractual relationship or secondary provider failover |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Complete dependency on single subservice organization (Supabase)** with carve-out treatment — all physical security and data center controls rely on one vendor
- **No WAF mentioned** despite being a web-based AI platform serving enterprise clients
- **No SIEM tool named** — monitoring tooling appears informal despite control testing
- **Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria explicitly excluded** — material gap for AI platform handling advisory firms' sensitive financial/business data

### YELLOW FLAGS
- No CI/CD pipeline or IaC tool named by product name
- RTO and RPO not disclosed
- No multi-region failover capability
- Audit period is only 3 months — relatively short observation window for Type 2
- No CDN mentioned despite SaaS delivery model
- Source control and CI/CD tools unnamed

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Zero exceptions** across all controls tested in the 3-month Type 2 period
- Report properly signed by Cody Chang (President and Co-Founder) — no placeholder text
- **Real network diagram** provided showing GCP Kubernetes architecture (not sample placeholder)
- Kubernetes container orchestration visible — indicates containerized deployment maturity
- Annual external penetration testing confirmed tested
- Monthly vulnerability scanning confirmed tested
- MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access confirmed tested
- Quarterly user access reviews confirmed tested
- Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed tested
- Cybersecurity insurance maintained
- BCDR plan tested across multiple loss scenarios vs. annual tabletop only
- Branch protection and code review required prior to production deployment confirmed tested
- Access revocation within one business day of termination confirmed tested
- IT Leadership Committee (ITLC) meets weekly for operational oversight
- Infrastructure-as-code with rollback capability confirmed tested
- Incident response plan tested periodically (CC7.3.3)

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

KRC Focus Inc. demonstrates ** stronger operational maturity than typical startup-stage AI companies** through zero control exceptions, containerized Kubernetes architecture, weekly ITLC governance, and comprehensive BCDR testing. The real network diagram and proper management attestation (signed by President/Co-Founder Cody Chang) indicate professionalism absent in comparable reports.

However, the **severe vendor concentration risk (2/10)** represents a material architectural limitation. Complete dependency on Supabase as a carve-out subservice organization — with all GCP services accessed indirectly — creates unacceptable single-point-of-failure exposure for enterprise workloads. The absence of WAF, unnamed SIEM, and exclusion of Processing Integrity/Privacy criteria further limit assurance coverage for an AI platform handling sensitive advisory data.

**Conditions for advancement:** Require documented multi-cloud roadmap (secondary cloud provider or direct GCP contractual relationship), implementation of WAF and named SIEM solution, and inclusion of Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria in the next audit cycle before significant capital deployment. Current posture acceptable for smaller pilot investments with strict vendor risk monitoring.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Valence Vibrations, Inc.
**Date:** September 17, 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 1 (As of September 17, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified

## Company Overview
| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Legal Name** | Valence Vibrations, Inc. |
| **Product** | Valence AI (AI-driven voice intelligence platform for real-time emotion detection and call analytics) |
| **HQ** | 7190 W Sunset Blvd #14, Los Angeles, CA 90046 |
| **Cloud** | AWS (Multi-AZ, regions unspecified) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure (6/10)
- Multi-AZ AWS deployment with VPC segmentation and dedicated API gateway
- VPN bastion, IDS/IPS, and IaC-based configuration management with rollback
- **Gaps:** No CDN, no multi-region failover, AWS regions not named

### App Architecture (5/10)
- Four distinct environments (Dev/QA/Staging/Prod) with branch protection
- MFA enforced on source code repository
- **Gaps:** Programming languages, frameworks, and container technology entirely undisclosed

### Data Layer (4/10)
- Per-VPC databases with multi-AZ placement and per-tenant segregation
- Documented data classification and retention policies
- **Gaps:** Database engine unnamed, no caching layer, backup frequency unspecified, no data warehouse

### Security (7/10)
- Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; monthly automated vuln scans
- IDS/IPS, firewall, anti-virus with continuous scanning, content filtering via proxy
- Quarterly access reviews and 1-day access revocation SLA
- **Gaps:** No WAF, no SIEM named, no cyber insurance disclosed

### DevOps (5/10)
- IaC configuration management and emergency change process
- Monthly patch reviews; separate environments maintained
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tool unnamed, source control platform unnamed, no containerization

### BCDR (6/10)
- Documented BCDR policy tested annually across multiple loss scenarios
- Multi-AZ failover (servers deployed across all availability zones)
- **Gaps:** No RTO/RPO stated, no multi-region disaster recovery

### Vendor Diversity (3/10)
- Extreme single-vendor concentration: AWS sole provider for compute, storage, IDS/IPS, and KMS
- No secondary cloud or subservice organizations disclosed

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **AWS** | Critical | Cloud infrastructure, data center, hosting, IDS/IPS, KMS | **Extreme concentration**; single point of failure for all operations and security tools |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Extreme vendor concentration:** AWS is the only named subservice organization; no secondary cloud or infrastructure vendor diversity
- **No RTO/RPO disclosed:** Disaster recovery objectives undefined despite real-time voice processing requirements
- **No WAF:** API-first product exposed to external callers without Web Application Firewall
- **Opaque toolchain:** CI/CD platform, source control system, programming languages, and frameworks entirely undisclosed in audit documentation
- **Automated auditor:** Accorp Partners operates an AI-assisted compliance platform; report exhibits templated control language suggesting limited bespoke validation for a 5-person team

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Key person risk:** 6-person team (CEO, CTO, 3 engineers, 1 EA) with CTO Shannon Brownlee serving as both signing authority and security owner
- **No CDN:** Voice/API workloads lack edge delivery optimization
- **Undefined backups:** Database backup frequency not specified
- **No SIEM:** Security alerting tools generically described without named SIEM solution
- **Point-in-time audit:** SOC 2 Type 1 provides design-only assurance (not operational effectiveness over time)
- **Sensitive data handling:** Processes enterprise call audio data with no specific data residency or encryption-in-transit standards disclosed

### GREEN FLAGS
- Annual external penetration testing and monthly automated vulnerability scanning
- Multi-AZ deployment with failover across all availability zones
- Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
- One-business-day access revocation SLA upon termination
- MFA enforced on production admin accounts and source code repository
- IaC-based configuration management with rollback capability
- Content filtering and anti-virus with continuous scanning on production servers

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

The overall score of **5/10** reflects a baseline-compliant but architecturally immature early-stage engineering organization. While security controls (7/10) and network segmentation exceed expectations for a 5-person team, **material risks persist**: extreme AWS vendor lock-in creates single-point-of-failure exposure, undisclosed RTO/RPO limits enterprise readiness, and the opaque technology stack (languages, frameworks, CI/CD) hinders maintainability assessment. The SOC 2 Type 1 (point-in-time) from an automated compliance platform provides limited operational assurance. 

**Conditions for Investment:** Require disclosure of RTO/RPO <4 hours given real-time voice SLA expectations, implementation of a WAF, vendor diversification strategy (secondary cloud or DR site), and migration path to SOC 2 Type 2. Monitor key-person dependency on CTO closely. Suitable for seed/Series A investment with technical remediation covenants.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Lilee AI Inc.
**Date:** July 22, 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 1 (As of July 22, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified

## Company Overview
| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Legal Name** | Lilee AI Inc. |
| **Product** | Lilee |
| **HQ** | 100-7405 Rte Transcanadienne, Montreal QC H4T1Z2, Canada |
| **Cloud** | Supabase (Infrastructure Subservice) + Vercel (Edge/Frontend) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure (5/10)
- Multi-AZ via Supabase, CDN acceleration via Vercel Edge
- Infrastructure-as-Code (config-as-code) implemented
- **Critical Gap:** Entire stack delegated to Supabase as carve-out subservice; no direct cloud provider control
- No named region or multi-region capability; single subservice provider dependency

### App Architecture (6/10)
- Modern serverless: Next.js 14 on Vercel Edge, Supabase managed backend
- Mastra Cloud for AI agents/workflows; Clerk for OIDC/OAuth authentication
- Functional separation across specialized managed services
- **Limitations:** Team of 4 engineers (high bus factor); no container orchestration; framework diversity limited to Next.js

### Data Layer (5/10)
- Supabase Postgres with Row-Level Security (JWT-based) for per-tenant segregation
- Supabase Object Storage; Multi-AZ via subservice
- **Gaps:** No caching layer disclosed; no data warehouse; backup policy exists but frequency unspecified

### Security (7/10)
- Annual external penetration testing; monthly automated vulnerability scanning
- IDS/IPS via Supabase; KMS encryption; content filtering; antivirus (weekly servers, daily endpoints)
- MFA enforced for administrative access, source code, and email; quarterly access reviews; 1-day revocation SLA
- **Gaps:** No WAF named; no SIEM named; no cybersecurity insurance disclosed

### DevOps (5/10)
- Branch protection with mandatory approvals; separate dev/prod environments with testing gates
- Emergency change process documented; config-as-code with rollback capability
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tool, IaC tool, and source control tool names not disclosed; Vercel manages deployment pipeline implicitly

### BCDR (5/10)
- BCDR policy documented and tested annually across multiple loss scenarios
- Multi-AZ failover via Supabase
- **Gaps:** No RTO/RPO disclosed; no multi-region failover; backup frequency unspecified; complete dependency on Supabase DR capabilities

### Vendor Diversity (6/10)
- 5 critical vendors with clear functional separation (Supabase, Vercel, Clerk, Mastra, AcuityNXT)
- **Risk:** High concentration in Supabase (DB + Auth + Storage + Monitoring); AcuityNXT EHR integration is critical external dependency

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supabase** | Critical | Primary infrastructure subservice (Postgres, Auth/RLS, Object Storage, Monitoring, VPC, KMS) | Single subservice org; complete outage = Lilee outage; no direct cloud control |
| **Vercel** | Critical | Frontend hosting, CDN, Next.js 14 Edge runtime, serverless API routes | Additional critical path dependency |
| **Clerk** | High | Auth-as-a-Service (OIDC/OAuth user authentication) | Third-party auth availability risk |
| **Mastra Cloud** | High | AI Workflows, Agents, Async Jobs/Queues | AI platform dependency for core automation features |
| **AcuityNXT** | High | EHR Authorization API integration (HTTP) | Healthcare API availability affects core functionality |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Infrastructure Control:** Entire infrastructure delegated to Supabase as carve-out subservice; Lilee has zero direct cloud provider control
- **BCDR Maturity:** No RTO or RPO disclosed; recovery capabilities cannot be quantified
- **Security Tooling:** No WAF or SIEM named; web application attack surface and security event correlation capabilities unknown
- **Tooling Opacity:** CI/CD, IaC, and source control tool names not disclosed; quality and security of toolchain unverifiable
- **Audit Limitation:** SOC 2 Type 1 only (point-in-time); no operational testing period; controls not validated over time

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Team Size:** Engineering team of 4 (1 Founding Engineer, 1 Software Engineer, 1 Intern) creates significant single points of failure
- **Data Protection:** No backup frequency specified; data recovery window unknown
- **Performance:** No caching layer disclosed; potential bottleneck at scale
- **Resilience:** No multi-region failover; vulnerable to regional Supabase outages
- **Coverage:** No cybersecurity insurance mentioned; AcuityNXT dependency for EHR functionality
- **Data Integrity:** Metadata listed infrastructure provider as "Render" but actual provider is Supabase (discrepancy in company-provided data)

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Security Posture:** Annual third-party penetration testing; monthly vulnerability scanning; zero exceptions across 29 tested controls
- **Access Controls:** MFA enforced for all admin access, source code, and email; quarterly user access reviews; 1-day access revocation SLA
- **Change Management:** Branch protection with mandatory approvals; separate environments with testing gates; emergency change process documented
- **Monitoring:** IDS/IPS active; antivirus maintained (weekly server definitions, daily endpoint definitions); content filtering via proxy
- **Architecture:** Modern auth (Clerk OIDC/OAuth); Row-Level Security for tenant segregation; config-as-code with rollback capability
- **Governance:** BCDR plan tested annually across multiple loss scenarios; Supabase SOC 2 report reviewed annually

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE-HIGH**

Lilee AI Inc. demonstrates a modern, AI-native serverless architecture with strong security controls (7/10) and clean SOC 2 Type 1 compliance (Unqualified opinion). However, the **complete delegation of infrastructure to Supabase as a carve-out subservice** creates critical concentration risk with no direct cloud control or multi-region resilience. Combined with a lean 4-person engineering team and undisclosed RTO/RPO metrics, the operational risk profile is elevated. The architecture is appropriate for a startup but requires monitoring of vendor concentration and team scalability as the platform grows. Clarification recommended on the "Render" vs. "Supabase" metadata discrepancy and backup frequency specifications.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Claim Health Inc.
**Date:** July 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 2 (April 7, 2025 - July 7, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified

## Company Overview
| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Legal Name** | Claim Health Inc. |
| **Product** | Claim Health RCM Platform (Revenue Cycle Management for post-acute care) |
| **HQ** | Not disclosed (fully remote; no physical office) |
| **Cloud** | Supabase (Single Provider) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure: 4/10
- Single cloud provider (Supabase) with multi-AZ deployment; no named regions or CDN
- No Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) tool named
- Heavy reliance on single subservice organization (Supabase) with carve-out treatment creates concentration risk
- Network diagram provided is an explicit sample/placeholder, not actual infrastructure

### Application Architecture: 3/10
- No languages, frameworks, or compute types disclosed in report
- Separate environments confirmed; branch protection enforced
- Architectural pattern unspecified; extremely limited technical disclosure

### Data Layer: 4/10
- Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration; per-tenant segregation confirmed
- Data classification and retention policies in place
- Only Supabase named as data store; no caching, data warehouse, or file storage layers disclosed

### Security: 6/10
- MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access; quarterly access reviews including privileged accounts
- IDS/IPS, firewalls, anti-malware on all systems, and log protection implemented
- Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; monthly vulnerability scanning
- Cybersecurity insurance maintained
- **Gaps:** WAF not mentioned; SIEM tool unnamed

### DevOps: 5/10
- Branch protection and mandatory code review enforced; separate environments maintained
- Emergency change process documented; configuration management tool for baseline/rollback
- Automatic patching confirmed
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tool, IaC tool, and source control tool not named

### Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery: 6/10
- BCDR policy documented, annually reviewed, and tested
- Daily backups with multi-AZ failover capability
- **Gaps:** No Recovery Time Objective (RTO) or Recovery Point Objective (RPO) stated; no multi-region failover capability disclosed

### Vendor Diversity: 2/10
- Extreme single-vendor concentration: Supabase is the sole named provider for all cloud and data center services
- No secondary cloud provider or CDN mentioned; all physical security controls outsourced to subservice organization

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Supabase** | Critical | Data center/cloud infrastructure (database, networking, servers, VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security) | **Extreme concentration risk**; carve-out treatment means physical security and availability zone controls are unaudited within this report and rely entirely on Supabase's external SOC 2; single point of failure for all infrastructure |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Report Integrity:** Management assertion section contains unfilled signature placeholders ("Your Name Here," "Signing Authority Name," "[Today's Date]"); report submitted unsigned
- **Template Errors:** System description sections contain highlighted yellow template instructions that were not deleted before submission; company address listed as "[Company Address]" placeholder
- **Documentation Gaps:** Network diagram is explicitly a sample/placeholder—not the actual infrastructure diagram
- **Vendor Lock-in:** Complete dependency on a single subservice organization (Supabase) with carve-out treatment; all physical security and data center controls are outsourced and unaudited in this report

### YELLOW FLAGS
- No Web Application Firewall (WAF) mentioned despite being a web-based platform processing healthcare data
- No SIEM tool named; security monitoring appears informal
- No CI/CD pipeline or IaC tooling named; limited transparency into deployment automation
- RTO and RPO not disclosed; no multi-region failover capability
- Trust criteria limited to **Security only**—Availability, Confidentiality, Privacy, and Processing Integrity all excluded from audit scope
- Audit period is only 3 months (minimum for Type 2), providing limited evidence of sustained control operation over time

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Zero exceptions** across all controls tested during the 3-month Type 2 period
- Annual external penetration testing and monthly vulnerability scanning confirmed operational
- Strong access controls: MFA enforced, quarterly access reviews, access revocation within one business day of termination
- BCDR plan tested annually; daily backups with multi-AZ confirmed
- Cybersecurity insurance maintained; branch protection and code review enforced

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: LOW**

**Rationale:**  
With an overall score of **4/10**, Claim Health presents significant governance and architectural risks. The unsigned SOC 2 report containing unfilled template placeholders raises serious questions about organizational maturity and attention to compliance detail—particularly concerning for a company that sells compliance automation tools. Extreme vendor concentration (2/10) with Supabase as the sole infrastructure provider creates existential business continuity risk, while the absence of basic architectural disclosures (languages, frameworks, CDN, WAF) limits technical due diligence capability. 

While security controls demonstrate moderate operational effectiveness (zero exceptions, annual pen testing, MFA), the combination of single-vendor dependency, incomplete documentation, and a minimal 3-month audit window indicates immature operational resilience. The carve-out treatment of Supabase means critical physical and environmental controls are unaudited within this report. Investment should be deferred pending remediation of report integrity issues, diversification of cloud infrastructure, and disclosure of RTO/RPO capabilities.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Chicory AI

| Karbonara, Inc. dba Chicory AI | Chicory AI | 400 6th Ln NE, Issaquah, WA 98029 | Amazon Web Services (AWS) |

### Infrastructure (6/10)

- Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; monthly automated vulnerability scanning
- IDS/IPS via AWS, network firewalls, and anti-virus on production servers with weekly definition updates
- MFA enforced for all administrative access and source code repository access; encryption at rest and in transit
- Cybersecurity insurance maintained and explicitly tested as a control (CC3.2)

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Salv AI Inc. (Tenzo)
**Date:** July 15, 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 2 (April 2, 2025 - July 2, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified*

## Company Overview
| Field | Details |
|-------|---------|
| **Legal Name** | Salv AI Inc. |
| **Product** | Tenzo (AI-automated compliance management platform) |
| **HQ** | Not Disclosed (Fully remote/cloud-native, no physical offices) |
| **Cloud** | AWS (Single provider, Multi-AZ) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure (6/10)
*   AWS-based multi-AZ deployment with VPC/ACL segmentation
*   Daily backups confirmed; automated patching reviewed monthly
*   **Gaps:** No multi-region failover disclosed; CDN not mentioned; specific AWS services (EC2, RDS, etc.) not named; Infrastructure-as-Code tool not identified

### App Architecture (4/10)
*   Environment separation confirmed (Production HA, Dev, Test, Demo)
*   Branch protection and mandatory code review enforced
*   **Gaps:** No programming languages, frameworks, or architecture patterns disclosed; extremely limited technical transparency

### Data Layer (5/10)
*   Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration; per-tenant data segregation implemented
*   Data classification policy exists; KMS encryption utilized
*   **Gaps:** Database technologies, caching layers, and storage services not named; no data warehouse mentioned

### Security (7/10)
*   Annual third-party penetration testing; monthly automated vulnerability scanning
*   IDS/IPS, endpoint AV, MFA enforcement across all critical systems, quarterly access reviews
*   Cybersecurity insurance maintained; AWS KMS encryption in transit and at rest
*   **Gaps:** No WAF explicitly mentioned; no SIEM or centralized logging platform named

### DevOps (5/10)
*   Emergency change management process documented; BCDR tested annually
*   Logical separation of environments maintained
*   **Gaps:** CI/CD pipeline tool not named; container orchestration platform not mentioned; IaC tooling not specified

### BCDR (6/10)
*   Documented BCDR policy reviewed annually; failover via multi-AZ server spin-up
*   Daily backup retention confirmed
*   **Gaps:** RTO/RPO metrics not disclosed; no multi-region disaster recovery capability

### Vendor Diversity (3/10)
*   AWS identified as sole subservice organization (carve-out)
*   **Gaps:** Single cloud provider with no backup/secondary vendor disclosed; report appears heavily template-based with circular dependencies (compliance vendor using own product template)

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | **Critical** | Primary cloud infrastructure, data center, compute, storage | **High** - Single vendor lock-in; no multi-region failover disclosed; subservice organization carve-out limits visibility into underlying controls |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
*   **Report Integrity:** Management assertion signature page submitted blank (unsigned); organization chart and network diagram are unmodified placeholders ("Sample network diagram - Please replace this...")  
*   **Template Contamination:** System description contains highlighted yellow template instructions that were not removed (e.g., "Instructions: Modify this text...")  
*   **Technical Opacity:** Zero disclosure of specific technology stack (no databases, languages, frameworks, CI/CD tools, or IaC platforms named)  
*   **Scope Contradiction:** Trust Services Criteria include Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy, but management determined these are "not relevant to the system" despite including them in scope  

### YELLOW FLAGS
*   **Vendor Concentration:** Only one subservice organization (AWS) identified; no other third-party vendors (SaaS, analytics, monitoring) disclosed  
*   **Missing Controls:** No WAF mentioned for web-facing SaaS platform; no CDN utilized; no SIEM or centralized monitoring tool named  
*   **BCDR Gaps:** RTO and RPO not quantified or disclosed  
*   **Audit Maturity:** First Type 2 report (minimum 3-month period); several controls marked "untestable" due to lack of triggering events (incidents, terminations) during short window  

### GREEN FLAGS
*   **Clean Audit:** Unqualified opinion with zero exceptions noted across all tested control activities  
*   **Security Posture:** Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; monthly automated vulnerability scans  
*   **Access Controls:** MFA enforced across production, source code, and email systems; quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts; access revocation within one business day of termination  
*   **Governance:** Risk and Governance Executive Committee established for oversight; cybersecurity insurance maintained  
*   **Data Protection:** Per-tenant data segregation implemented; daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed  

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: LOW**

While Salv AI maintains an unqualified SOC 2 Type 2 opinion and demonstrates baseline security hygiene (pen testing, MFA, encryption), the **overall technical maturity score of 5/10** combined with **critical report quality failures** (unsigned assertions, placeholder diagrams, template residue) raises significant concerns about operational rigor and transparency. 

The extreme lack of technical specificity—particularly for a compliance automation vendor itself—suggests either immature documentation practices or deliberate obfuscation. The circular use of their own product template without proper customization undermines confidence in their ability to manage complex compliance workflows for customers. 

**Recommendation:** Defer investment pending remediation of report deficiencies, disclosure of specific technology stack, establishment of multi-region BCDR, and subsequent audit period (6-12 months) demonstrating sustained control operation. Require signed management assertions and actual infrastructure diagrams before any technical review completion.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Explore Interfaces, Inc. (dba Context)

**Date:** July 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 2 (April 2, 2025 - July 2, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified  
*Signing Authority: Derek Hsieh, CPO*

## Company Overview

| Legal Name | Product | HQ | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| Explore Interfaces, Inc. (dba Context) | Context: AI-powered collaborative office suite (presentations, documents, spreadsheets) powered by proprietary "Context Engine" | Not disclosed (co-working space) | **Primary:** Vercel (Compute, CDN, Edge); **Data:** Supabase (PostgreSQL, Object Storage) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

### Infrastructure: 5/10
- **Extreme Vendor Concentration:** Vercel is the sole subservice organization for all compute (frontend + backend), CDN, and edge capabilities — single provider failure results in total platform outage
- **Resilience:** Native multi-AZ failover via Vercel availability zones; no multi-region deployment capability or secondary provider strategy
- **IaC:** Configuration-as-code referenced for baselines but specific tool (Terraform/Pulumi) unnamed
- **Platform Architecture:** PaaS/edge model purpose-built for Next.js workloads but offers less flexibility than hyperscale clouds (AWS/Azure/GCP)

### Application Architecture: 6/10

- **Governance:** Separate development/test/production environments with enforced branch protection; emergency change process documented

- **Storage:** Supabase Storage for file objects; daily backups tested with automated failure alerting
- **Resilience:** Multi-AZ configuration active; data classification and retention policies established
- **Gaps:** No caching layer (Redis) or data warehouse (Snowflake/BigQuery) named; Supabase as sole data provider creates concentration risk

### Security: 7/10

### Security (7/10)

- MFA enforced for admin access; quarterly access reviews; antivirus on all systems
- KMS encryption at rest; log protection; server hardening
- **Gaps:** WAF not mentioned; SIEM tool unnamed

### DevOps (6/10)
- ECS Fargate orchestration with IaC-style baseline configs and rollback capability
- Branch protection with required approvals; emergency change process; monthly patch reviews
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tooling and source control platform names undisclosed

### Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery (6/10)

- Multi-AZ failover explicitly described (cross-AZ server spin-up)

### Vendor Diversity (3/10)
- Single critical vendor (AWS) with no named alternatives or diversification strategy
- Complete infrastructure dependency on one provider

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | Critical | Cloud infrastructure, data center services, compute (ECS Fargate), networking, storage | **High** – Single point of failure; no geographic redundancy or provider diversification |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **SOC 2 Type 1 Limitation:** Controls designed but not operationally tested over a period; Type 2 required for operational effectiveness validation
- **Single Cloud Concentration:** AWS-only infrastructure with no named backup provider or multi-region failover
- **Missing Web Application Firewall:** No WAF documented despite web-facing application handling customer CI/CD integrations
- **Opaque Core Stack:** Database technologies, source control systems, and CI/CD tooling undisclosed
- **Supply Chain Exposure:** As a CI/CD-integrated testing platform with read access to customer applications, security of the integration path is not addressed; Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded from report

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Environment Segregation:** Network diagram shows staging and production within same VPC, suggesting immature isolation despite controls language
- **No CDN:** Content delivery strategy unspecified for globally-accessed platform
- **Undisclosed Recovery Metrics:** RTO and RPO targets not stated
- **Missing SIEM:** Logging exists but no Security Information and Event Management tool named
- **Boutique Auditor:** Accorp Partners is a small firm (PAC-FIRM-LIC-47383), not Big 4 or top-tier

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Clean Network Architecture:** VPC with segmented private subnets, ALB, ECS Fargate, NAT Gateway
- **Robust Security Testing:** Annual external penetration testing; monthly automated vulnerability scans
- **Strong Access Governance:** MFA for admin access; quarterly access reviews; branch protection with required approvals; access revoked within 1 business day of termination
- **Data Protection:** KMS-based at-rest encryption; per-tenant data segregation
- **Operational Discipline:** BCDR tested annually; IDS/IPS deployed; antivirus with weekly updates; IaC with rollback capability

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Loggia AI exhibits strong security hygiene (7/10) and solid cloud-native architecture, but the **5/10 overall score** reflects critical opacity in core infrastructure components and excessive vendor concentration (3/10). The recently attained **SOC 2 Type 1** status (October 2025) provides limited assurance as operational effectiveness remains unverified; Type 2 completion is essential.

The platform's unique supply-chain position—integrating into customer CI/CD pipelines with AI-driven application access—introduces unaddressed risks not covered by current audit scope (Privacy/Processing Integrity excluded). While technical controls are sound, the lack of WAF, CDN, multi-region strategy, and transparent technology disclosures indicates immaturity for enterprise-critical deployments.

**Conditions for Investment Advancement:**
1. **Type 2 Certification:** Achieve SOC 2 Type 2 with 6-12 months operational testing
2. **Infrastructure Hardening:** Implement WAF, disclose RTO/RPO, and establish multi-region failover or hot standby
3. **Stack Transparency:** Disclose database technologies, CI/CD architecture, and security controls for customer integration pathways
4. **Vendor Resilience:** Diversify critical dependencies or formalize failover strategy with secondary provider

The Type 1 report offers limited assurance (design-only, no operating effectiveness testing). Proceed with enhanced vendor due diligence, particularly regarding undisclosed technology dependencies and AI model risk management, contingent on commitments to improve architectural transparency and define resilience metrics before significant capital deployment.

## Risk Assessment

- **CI/CD Opacity:** No CI/CD tool named—cannot assess pipeline security, secret management, or automation maturity
- **Monitoring Gap:** No SIEM mentioned; log aggregation appears manual/alerting-only
- **Recovery Metrics Missing:** RTO and RPO not disclosed or tested

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Regional Risk:** Multi-region failover not implemented—single Supabase region (unspecified) creates geographic concentration risk
- **Edge Security:** No WAF explicitly mentioned (Cloudflare present but only referenced for JWT verification)
- **Transparency Gaps:** Source control system, programming languages, and frameworks not disclosed
- **Scope Limitation:** Trust criteria limited to Security only—Availability and Confidentiality not in scope (concerning for healthcare-adjacent voice data)
- **Access Management:** No SSO mentioned; relies on Supabase password policy infrastructure

### GREEN FLAGS

- **Architectural Transparency:** Clear network diagram published showing full AI inference pipeline
- **Insurance:** Cybersecurity insurance maintained with documented coverage
- **Change Control:** BCDR annually tested; quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Willow Care Inc. presents a **6/10** technology posture reflecting solid security fundamentals for a seed-stage YC company, undermined by critical infrastructure concentration risk. The modern AI pipeline architecture (Baseten + Groq) demonstrates sophisticated low-latency ML engineering, and the clean SOC 2 audit (zero exceptions) validates strong operational discipline. However, the carve-out of Supabase controls creates a significant audit gap—100% of infrastructure resides with a single unaudited provider. 

**Proceed with conditions:** Require disclosure of RTO/RPO metrics, implementation of multi-region failover, and visibility into CI/CD toolchain before Series A deployment. Suitable for investment with board-level oversight on infrastructure diversification and security telemetry gaps (SIEM/WAF). Not yet enterprise-ready for healthcare or regulated workloads requiring Availability/Confidentiality controls.

### Vendor Diversity: 3/10

- Minimal vendor stack; no SaaS productivity tools (Slack, GWorkspace) in scope
- Single point of failure if Render experiences outage or security incident

## Vendor Dependency Map

| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Render** | Critical | Cloud platform subservice organization; hosts 100% of production infrastructure (VPC, K8s, database, storage, backups, LB, monitoring) | Extreme concentration; no geographic redundancy; Render outage = Isoform outage |
| **Sophos** | High | Firewall and endpoint protection | Standard vendor dependency; partially mitigates endpoint risk |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **100% Infrastructure Dependency:** Complete reliance on Render as sole subservice organization with no architectural fallback or multi-region deployment
- **Opaque Data Layer:** Database engine undisclosed—impossible to assess security posture, patch status, or known vulnerabilities
- **Invisible Toolchain:** CI/CD and IaC tools unnamed; cannot verify pipeline integrity or configuration drift controls
- **Monitoring Gaps:** No SIEM or WAF named; log aggregation and deep inspection capabilities unverified
- **Single Region:** AWS us-west-2 only; no geographic redundancy for disaster recovery

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Undisclosed Recovery Metrics:** RTO and RPO not documented despite annual BCDR testing
- **LLM Pipeline Security:** AI code-generation layer (core to development) not discussed in SOC 2 scope; LLM supply chain risk unassessed
- **Short Audit Period:** 3-month operating history (March–June 2025) limits assessment of control consistency
- **No Customer SSO:** No SSO mentioned for customer-facing platform access
- **No CDN:** Potential performance and availability impacts for mid-market customers outside Bay Area
- **Untested Incident Controls:** Cybersecurity insurance and incident response procedures could not be validated due to zero security incidents in period

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Robust Testing Cadence:** Annual third-party penetration testing and monthly automated vulnerability scanning
- **AI-Enforced Quality Gates:** Static analysis mandatory via AI code-generation layer before TypeScript commits
- **Strong Access Controls:** MFA enforced; quarterly access reviews; 1-day access revocation; hardened Kubernetes runtime with secrets isolation
- **Proven BCDR Discipline:** Zero exceptions noted in all 57 testable controls; daily backups with multi-AZ; 24x7 SRE incident response with SLA-backed remediation

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Isoform Inc. demonstrates solid security hygiene for its stage (Security: 7/10) with disciplined access management and a clean SOC 2 report (zero exceptions). However, the **5/10 overall score reflects extreme vendor concentration (3/10)** and architectural opacity that materially constrain enterprise readiness. 

The company’s core IP—its Kubernetes integration runtime—is entirely captive to Render’s us-west-2 region, creating unmitigated single points of failure in infrastructure (compute, data, networking). Combined with undisclosed database engines and unnamed DevOps tooling, Isoform lacks the transparency required for high-confidence infrastructure risk assessment.

**Conditions for Improvement:**
1. **Diversify Infrastructure:** Implement multi-region deployment (secondary cloud or AWS region independent of Render) or negotiate contractual failover guarantees with Render
2. **Disclose Stack:** Identify database engine, CI/CD platform, and IaC tools to enable CVE tracking and pipeline security review
3. **Expand Observability:** Deploy SIEM/WAF and publish RTO/RPO metrics
4. **Mitigate Key-Person Risk:** Address apparent minimal staffing (CEO → Head of GTM only) with documented bus-factor mitigation and LLM pipeline security audit

**Proceed with caution:** Suitable for investment only if coupled with technical roadmap milestones addressing vendor concentration and architectural transparency within 6–12 months.

*   **Strengths:** Comprehensive control suite including Cloud Armor (WAF/DDoS), GCP IDS/IPS, annual third-party penetration testing, monthly automated vulnerability scanning, enforced MFA for all admin/production access, endpoint protection, GCP Security Command Center (SIEM), cybersecurity insurance, and quarterly privileged access reviews. ### Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery — 7/10
*   **Strengths:** BCDR policy documented and tested annually; DR procedures spin up multiple servers across all GCP availability zones; daily backups with multi-AZ resilience.
*   **Weaknesses:** No RTO/RPO targets disclosed; multi-region failover capabilities absent; recovery limited to multi-AZ within single cloud provider.

### Vendor Diversity — 4/10
*   **Critical Concentration Risk:** Near-total dependency on Google ecosystem (GCP + Firebase). All critical infrastructure (compute, storage, networking, security, CDN, logging) consolidated under single vendor. Only two subservice vendors identified, both Google subsidiaries.

## Vendor Dependency Map

| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| Google Cloud Platform (GCP) | **Critical** | Primary cloud infrastructure, data center services, compute, storage, networking, security services (Cloud Armor, SCC), CDN | Single-vendor lock-in; no multi-region failover; concentration risk |
| Firebase (Google) | **High** | Firebase Hosting, Firebase Realtime Database | Same vendor ecosystem; shared fate risk with GCP |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
*   **Extreme Vendor Concentration:** Near-total dependency on Google (GCP + Firebase) with no disclosed multi-cloud strategy or multi-region DR architecture.
*   **Missing DR Metrics:** RTO and RPO not disclosed despite annual BCDR testing; reliance on multi-AZ only within single region.
*   **Documentation Gaps:** CI/CD tooling and programming languages not named in audit report, limiting technical transparency.

### YELLOW FLAGS
*   **Regional Resilience Gap:** No multi-region failover capability; DR limited to multi-AZ within GCP only.
*   **Architecture Omissions:** No caching layer or data warehouse mentioned in system description.
*   **Access Control:** SSO implementation for customer-facing access not confirmed.
*   **Audit Anomaly:** Audit period dates (Feb 2015 - May 2025) span 10 years—likely typographical error for Feb 2025 start date, suggesting potential documentation quality issues.

### GREEN FLAGS
*   **Robust Security Testing:** Annual external penetration testing by independent third parties; monthly automated vulnerability scans.
*   **Strong Access Controls:** MFA enforced for all admin/production access and source code repositories; quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts; access revocation within one business day of termination.
*   **Encryption & Monitoring:** CMEK encryption for cloud storage; Cloud Armor DDoS protection; Identity-Aware Proxy; GCP Security Command Center (SIEM) deployed.
*   **Governance Maturity:** Risk and Governance Executive Committee with semiannual oversight meetings; weekly IT Leadership Committee—unusually mature for startup stage.
*   **Compliance Cleanliness:** Zero exceptions across all tested controls; explicit carve-out of physical/environmental controls (CC6.4, A1.2) to GCP/building management; cybersecurity insurance maintained.

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Pensieve Inc. demonstrates strong security controls (8/10) and mature governance for its stage, but faces significant concentration risk with near-total GCP dependency and lack of multi-region resilience. The undisclosed RTO/RPO metrics and single-cloud architecture present material continuity risks for an educational platform handling sensitive student data. Investment is viable contingent upon establishment of multi-region DR capabilities, disclosure of recovery metrics, and diversification of critical vendor dependencies. The anomalous audit period dates should be clarified with management prior to closing.

- ** Strengths:** Documented BCDR policy reviewed and tested annually across multiple loss scenarios; multi-AZ failover via AWS; business impact analysis referenced
- ** Gaps:** No RTO/RPO disclosed; no multi-region failover; backup frequency unspecified; DR relies entirely on AZ-level redundancy within us-east-1

### Vendor Diversity — 5/10
- ** Strengths:** Three vendors provide functional separation: AWS (infrastructure), PlanetScale (database via PrivateLink), Hatchet (workflow orchestration)
- ** Gaps:** Heavy AWS concentration; PlanetScale itself runs on AWS, limiting true cloud provider diversity

## Vendor Dependency Map

| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** | Critical | Primary cloud infrastructure — EKS, EC2, Aurora DB, VPC networking, KMS, IAM, multi-AZ | Single region (us-east-1) concentration; regional outage would impact service |
| **PlanetScale** | Critical | Managed MySQL database — primary data store connected via AWS PrivateLink | Runs on AWS underlying infrastructure; represents single point of failure for transactional data |
| **Hatchet** | High | Workflow orchestration engine (Hatchet Engine + Worker + Frontend) running as dedicated EKS pods | Embedded open-source dependency; functional complexity |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **No RTO/RPO disclosed** — BCDR maturity cannot be quantified; recovery capabilities unverified
- **No WAF named** — web application attack surface for AI API platform unclear
- **No SIEM named** — security event correlation and centralized logging capability unknown
- **Undisclosed tooling** — CI/CD, IaC, and source control tool names not provided; quality unverifiable
- **Scope limitation** — SOC 2 covers Security trust criteria only; Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy excluded despite handling customer support data under GDPR jurisdiction
- **Backup opacity** — backup frequency not specified; data recovery exposure unknown

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Single region dependency** — AWS us-east-1 only; no multi-region failover despite multi-AZ resilience
- **False diversity** — PlanetScale runs on AWS; PrivateLink provides network isolation but not provider redundancy
- **Performance gaps** — No CDN mentioned; no caching layer explicitly disclosed for AI workloads
- **Insurance gap** — No cybersecurity insurance mentioned
- **Template artifacts** — Yellow highlighting in system description section suggests workflow formatting issues (though technical content appears genuine)
- **Privacy exclusion** — Danish company (GDPR jurisdiction) yet Privacy trust criteria excluded from SOC 2 scope

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Kubernetes maturity** — EKS container orchestration significantly more advanced than typical early-stage AI startup
- **Observability** — Grafana monitoring deployed as dedicated infrastructure component with custom network diagram showing pod-level detail
- **Workflow architecture** — Hatchet orchestration engine with dedicated Engine/Worker/Frontend pods; Procrastinate worker for async processing indicates sophisticated job queue design
- **Secure connectivity** — PlanetScale via AWS PrivateLink eliminates internet-exposed database access
- **Data separation** — Aurora DB for task queue distinct from PlanetScale primary store
- **Security hygiene** — Zero exceptions across 29 tested controls; annual external pen testing; monthly vuln scans; quarterly access reviews; 1-day revocation SLA
- **Access controls** — MFA enforced for all administrative access, source code, and email; branch protection with mandatory approvals
- **BCDR process** — Annual testing across multiple loss scenarios; multi-AZ failover active
- **Vendor management** — AWS SOC 2 reviewed annually; quarterly vendor risk assessments for non-SOC vendors

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE-HIGH**

Parahelp demonstrates **solid architectural maturity** (7/10 overall) with enterprise-grade Kubernetes orchestration, sophisticated workflow engine design, and strong security hygiene. The unqualified SOC 2 opinion with zero control exceptions validates operational discipline. However, **single-region concentration** (us-east-1), **undisclosed backup specifics**, and **limited SOC 2 scope** (Security-only) introduce measurable risk.

**Conditions precedent:** Disclose RTO/RPO targets, backup frequency, and critical tooling stack (CI/CD, IaC, SCM); implement multi-region DR or justify single-region acceptance; expand SOC 2 scope to include Availability and Privacy given GDPR jurisdiction. Suitable for investment with standard technical diligence conditions.

*   **Security Program:** Annual independent pen testing with remediation tracking; monthly automated vulnerability scanning; encryption at rest and in transit
*   **Change Management:** Branch protection requiring additional approvals for production; formal emergency change process; IaC with rollback
*   **Data Governance:** Per-tenant data segregation confirmed; formal data retention and secure disposal policies; BCDR tested annually across scenarios

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

The unqualified SOC 2 opinion and foundational security controls provide baseline assurance, but heavy vendor concentration, architectural opacity, and absence of key security layers (WAF, SSO) require remediation before scaling. Suitable for investment with contingency provisions for Type 2 completion, infrastructure diversification, and detailed architecture review. The "AI-native" positioning lacks technical substantiation in current documentation.

- **Weaknesses:** No stated RTO/RPO targets; no multi-region disaster recovery; limited procedural detail on specific recovery steps; 3-month audit period provides no evidence of BCDR execution under actual stress

### Vendor Diversity: 3/10
- **Assessment:** Extreme vendor concentration with AWS as the sole named infrastructure provider; no disclosed supplementary services, alternatives, or multi-cloud strategy; vendor management policy exists but actual third-party ecosystem remains opaque beyond the single critical dependency

## Vendor Dependency Map

| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| AWS | Critical | Cloud infrastructure, compute (Lambda/ECS/EC2), storage (S3), CDN (CloudFront), CI/CD (CodePipeline) | **Severe**: Single vendor lock-in; no multi-region; no disclosed alternatives; complete platform dependency |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Extreme Vendor Concentration:** AWS is the only named infrastructure vendor with no disclosed alternatives or multi-region strategy; represents a single point of failure for the entire platform
- **First-Time Audit Indicators:** Very short 3-month audit period (May-July 2025) suggests inaugural SOC 2 engagement with limited operational history review
- **Missing BCDR Metrics:** No RTO/RPO disclosed despite having a BCDR policy; no multi-region failover capability documented
- **Opaque Data Architecture:** No specific database technologies, caching layers, or AI/ML infrastructure disclosed despite handling customer data and claiming "AI-native" status
- **Un testable Controls:** Several controls (cyber insurance claims process, incident response, termination procedures) were untestable due to zero events occurring during the abbreviated 3-month window

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Excluded Trust Criteria:** Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded as "not relevant" — unusual for a platform processing customer data with natural language inputs
- **Missing WAF:** No Web Application Firewall mentioned despite being a public-facing tool builder platform
- **Template Artifacts:** Report appears prepared from audit firm template with minimal customization; references to "Windows/Network team" incongruent with cloud-native architecture
- **Fast Turnaround:** Report signed August 19, 2025 (19 days after period end) suggests limited review time or immature control environment
- **Organizational Maturity:** Org chart shows only CEO → CTO → Engineering Team; extremely lean structure with no physical office (fully remote/cloud-only)

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Clean Opinion:** Unqualified opinion with zero exceptions across all tested controls
- **Strong Access Controls:** MFA enforced universally; quarterly user access reviews; one business day SLA for access revocation
- **Security Hygiene:** Annual third-party penetration testing; monthly vulnerability scanning; formal change management with branch protection
- **Data Governance:** Per-tenant data segregation; data classification policy; daily backups with multi-AZ and alerting
- **Governance Cadence:** Weekly IT Leadership Committee meetings; semiannual RGEC meetings; documented policies for all major domains

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Olive Financial presents a **moderate risk** technical profile suitable for investment with significant contingencies. While the company demonstrates foundational security hygiene (clean SOC 2 opinion, MFA, encryption, pen testing) and cloud-native cost efficiency (AWS-only, no physical office), the **5/10 overall score reflects dangerous opacity in the technology stack and extreme vendor concentration**.

The absence of multi-region strategy, unidentified database technologies, and lack of AI-specific infrastructure disclosure for an "AI-native" company raises fundamental questions about technical differentiation and operational resilience. The 3-month audit window provides insufficient evidence of sustained control operation under stress.

**Conditions for Investment:**
1. Disclosure of specific database technologies, caching strategies, and AI/ML serving infrastructure
2. Establishment of multi-region failover with documented RTO/RPO targets
3. Diversification of critical vendor dependencies (monitoring, backup, security tools beyond AWS native)
4. Completion of a 12-month SOC 2 audit period to validate control sustainability

Proceed with technical validation focused on architecture review and BCDR testing prior to closing.

# Tech Due Diligence Report: Cluely, Inc.
**Date:** July 9, 2025 | **Source:** SOC 2 Type 1 (As of June 27, 2025) | **Auditor:** Accorp Partners | **Opinion:** Unqualified

## Company Overview

| Legal Name | Product | HQ | Cloud |
|------------|---------|-----|-------|
| Cluely, Inc. | Cluely (AI Desktop Assistant for Video Conferencing Coaching) | 520 Bryant St, San Francisco CA 94107 | Multi-Cloud: Vercel (Primary), AWS (K8s), Cloudflare (Edge), Neon (DB) |

## Technology Stack Assessment

**Overall Score: 6/10**

### Infrastructure (7/10)
- Highest vendor diversity in cohort: Vercel + Cloudflare + AWS + Neon explicitly architected
- Multi-AZ Vercel deployment confirmed; no multi-region failover
- Architectural sophistication: Cloudflare Workers for async queue processing, AWS Kubernetes for AI compute
- **Gap:** AWS and Cloudflare not acknowledged as formal subservice organizations despite critical infrastructure roles

### App Architecture (7/10)
- Most technically detailed network diagram in cohort: Vercel API platform → Cloudflare Workers (Queue 1/2) → AWS K8s cluster → Neon DB
- Kubernetes (AWS) confirmed with separate environments branch protection enforced
- Sophisticated edge computing pattern via Cloudflare Workers for asynchronous processing
- **Gap:** Programming languages/frameworks not specified despite high infrastructure specificity

### Data Layer (4/10)
- Neon (serverless Postgres) explicitly named as production database—unique positive signal in cohort
- Data classification and retention policies documented; multi-AZ confirmed via Vercel failover
- **Gaps:** No explicit backup frequency stated (SOC 2 Type 1 lacks CC7.4.2 control), no caching layer or file storage named, single database technology dependency

### Security (6/10)
- Annual external penetration testing, monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plans
- IDS/IPS via Vercel, KMS encryption at rest, MFA enforced across platform/source code/email
- Quarterly access reviews, next-business-day revocation, content filtering with email scanning
- **Gaps:** No WAF explicitly mentioned, no named SIEM, no cybersecurity insurance disclosed

### DevOps (6/10)
- Branch protection enforced, emergency change process documented, monthly patch reviews with automatic patching
- BCDR tested annually with multiple loss scenarios; configuration management confirmed
- **Gaps:** CI/CD tool not named, IaC tool not specified; multi-provider architecture (4 vendors) requires higher maturity to operate than currently evidenced

### BCDR (4/10)
- BCDR policy documented, reviewed annually, tested across multiple loss scenarios
- Multi-AZ failover via Vercel availability zone spinning
- **Gaps:** No RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, no explicit daily backup frequency stated

### Vendor Diversity (8/10)
- Five named critical providers: Vercel, Cloudflare, AWS, Neon, Deepgram
- Multi-vendor architecture significantly reduces single-vendor dependency risk vs. cohort peers
- Only Vercel formally scoped as subservice organization with own SOC 2 report

## Vendor Dependency Map

| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| **Vercel** | Critical | Primary cloud infrastructure (hosting, VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security) | **Mitigated:** Own SOC 2 Type 2 (carve-out) |
| **Cloudflare** | Critical | CDN/proxy layer and edge computing (Workers for async queues 1/2) | **High:** Not formal subservice org despite critical data routing/compute |
| **AWS** | Critical | Kubernetes cluster hosting Deepgram AI engines (speech processing) | **High:** Not formal subservice org; hosts core AI compute |
| **Neon** | Critical | Serverless Postgres database (production data store) | **Moderate:** Not formal subservice org; single DB technology |
| **Deepgram** | Critical | AI speech-to-text engine (3 instances: Engine, API, License) | **High:** Not formal subservice org; core product dependency |

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **SOC 2 Type 1 Only:** Point-in-time design assessment only; operating effectiveness not tested (no Type 2)
- **Backup Frequency Undefined:** No explicit daily backup frequency stated for critical Neon database
- **Insurance Gap:** No cybersecurity insurance mentioned (unlike cohort peers)
- **Subservice Organization Scoping Failure:** AWS Kubernetes cluster and Cloudflare not listed as formal subservice organizations despite handling critical AI processing and data routing

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Incomplete Vendor Scoping:** Only Vercel formally scoped; Neon and Deepgram also excluded from subservice organization list
- **Security Visibility Gaps:** No WAF explicitly mentioned (relying on implied Vercel edge protection), no named SIEM
- **BCDR Metrics Missing:** RTO and RPO not disclosed; multi-region failover not implemented
- **DevOps Tooling Opaque:** CI/CD and IaC tools not named in report
- **Deepgram Concentration Risk:** AI speech engine not scoped as subservice organization; any Deepgram outage likely breaks core product functionality

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Architectural Transparency:** Most technically specific network diagram in cohort—five named services (Vercel, Cloudflare Workers, Neon, AWS K8s, Deepgram) explicitly mapped
- **Vendor Diversification:** Only cohort company with explicit CDN (Cloudflare), edge compute (Workers), named AI provider (Deepgram), and serverless Postgres (Neon)
- **Security Fundamentals:** Annual external pen testing, monthly vulnerability scanning, MFA enforced universally, quarterly access reviews
- **Operational Maturity:** BCDR tested annually with multiple scenarios; CISO role explicitly defined; vendor management program includes annual SOC reviews
- **Vercel Compliance:** Subservice organization maintains own SOC 2 report (carve-out properly executed)

## Recommendation

**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Cluely presents a technically sophisticated, multi-cloud architecture (6/10) with exceptional vendor diversity and detailed infrastructure transparency that reduces single-provider concentration risk. However, the SOC 2 Type 1 limitation—absent operating effectiveness testing—combined with undefined backup frequencies, lack of cybersecurity insurance, and incomplete subservice organization scoping (AWS/Cloudflare/Deepgram/Neon not formally acknowledged) creates material control gaps for a B2B SaaS platform processing sensitive sales conversations. 

**Immediate Preconditions:** Completion of SOC 2 Type 2 (minimum 6-month observation period), documentation of RTO/RPO and daily backup frequencies, procurement of cyber insurance, and formal scoping of AWS and Cloudflare as subservice organizations.

**Suitable for:** Growth equity with strong technical advisory capacity to remediate control gaps prior to enterprise scale. Not recommended for passive investment until Type 2 completion.

## Risk Assessment

### RED FLAGS
- **Complete AWS Lock-in:** Single-vendor dependency with no secondary cloud provider or CDN creates existential vendor concentration risk
- **Technology Opacity:** No disclosure of database technologies, programming languages, frameworks, or core dependencies prevents architectural validation
- **Tooling Darkness:** Absence of named CI/CD platforms, source control systems, monitoring/APM, or SIEM tools despite claimed controls
- **Point-in-Time Only:** SOC 2 Type 1 (design only) vs. Type 2 (operating effectiveness); no historical control testing over time
- **Template-Driven Report:** Significant verbatim repetition of control descriptions across criteria suggests compliance automation boilerplate rather than customized assessment

- **Missing Edge Security:** No WAF explicitly mentioned despite firewalls and IDS/IPS presence
- **Undefined Resilience Metrics:** BCDR policy exists but lacks quantified RTO/RPO targets
- **Backup Ambiguity:** Formal backup policy present but frequency not specified
- **Enterprise Readiness Gaps:** No SSO capability mentioned; TLS version unspecified (potential legacy protocol risk)
- **Exclusion Scope:** Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded for an email infrastructure platform handling message content
- **Early-Stage Concentration:** 3-person C-suite (CEO, CTO, COO) with no physical office; fully remote cloud-native operation

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Geographic Resilience:** Multi-region AWS deployment (us-west-1 + us-east-1) with multi-AZ for startup-stage company
- **Identity Security:** MFA enforced across production admin, email, and source code access; quarterly access reviews
- **Data Segregation:** Per-tenant data isolation built into platform architecture
- **Security Testing:** Annual third-party penetration testing; monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
- **Operational Maturity:** IaC with rollback capability; branch protection; documented BCDR with annual testing
- **Clean Audit:** Unqualified auditor opinion with zero exceptions; 12-day turnaround suggests efficient compliance infrastructure

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

AgentMail, Inc. presents a **6/10** technology risk profile with strong foundational infrastructure (multi-region AWS, SOC 2 Type 1) offset by critical vendor concentration and concerning opacity in core technology choices. While the platform demonstrates compliance-forward positioning appropriate for enterprise sales, the complete dependency on AWS without secondary providers, combined with undisclosed database technologies and generic tooling descriptions, introduces material architectural and business continuity risks. 

**Conditions for advancement:** 
1. Disclose core technology stack (languages, databases, frameworks) for architectural review
2. Establish RTO/RPO targets and quantify backup frequencies
3. Develop multi-cloud or multi-vendor strategy to mitigate AWS concentration risk
4. Obtain SOC 2 Type 2 to demonstrate sustained control effectiveness over time
5. Implement WAF and confirm TLS 1.2+ minimum standards

Suitable for investment with technical escrow requirements and milestone-based transparency commitments prior to Series B scale.

- **Supply chain risk:** Baseten and Groq are relatively small/niche AI inference providers with unclear redundancy mechanisms
- Vendor management policy requires annual SOC report reviews

## Vendor Dependency Map
| Vendor | Criticality | Purpose | Risk |
|--------|-------------|---------|------|
| Supabase | Critical | Primary database, backend infrastructure, authentication, data center services (carve-out subservice org) | **High** - Single point of failure for entire backend; controls outside audit scope |
| Baseten | Critical | ML model hosting for voice-to-text transcription | **High** - Niche provider, unclear failover/redundancy |
| Groq | Critical | LLM inference for formatting transcriptions | **High** - Niche provider, supply chain concentration with Baseten |
| Cloudflare | Critical | JWT verification gateway / API security layer | **Medium** - Used only as auth gateway, not full CDN |

## Risk Assessment
### RED FLAGS
- **Carve-Out Concentration:** Heavy reliance on Supabase as subservice organization means significant infrastructure controls (physical security, network security, system operations) are outside the audit scope and not independently tested
- **AI Pipeline Fragility:** Critical voice processing pipeline depends on two niche vendors (Baseten, Groq) with no disclosed redundancy or failover mechanisms if either becomes unavailable
- **Undefined Recovery Metrics:** BCDR policy exists but lacks disclosed RTO/RPO targets; backup frequency unspecified despite formal policy
- **Technical Opacity:** Unusually low technical specificity—no CI/CD, source control, IaC, or monitoring tools named anywhere in the report
- **Single Region:** No multi-region failover capability; single AZ failure within Supabase region is the only covered scenario

### YELLOW FLAGS
- **Type 1 Limitation:** Point-in-time design assessment only (August 14, 2025); no operating effectiveness testing over a period (Type 2 not yet achieved)
- **Missing Security Controls:** No WAF mentioned despite processing audio over internet; no SIEM named despite logging references; no cybersecurity insurance disclosed
- **Privacy Exclusion:** Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria explicitly excluded despite handling voice/audio data containing PII
- **Template Artifacts:** Report contains generic boilerplate language (e.g., "Windows/Network team" for patch management in a Mac-focused company) suggesting insufficient customization
- **Team Size Risk:** YC-backed company with only CEO/CTO roles in org chart indicates high key-person dependency

### GREEN FLAGS
- **Clean Audit:** Unqualified opinion with zero exceptions or qualifications from Accorp Partners
- **Strong Access Governance:** MFA enforced for production, source code, and email; quarterly user access reviews; termination access revocation within one business day
- **Security Testing Discipline:** Annual independent penetration testing and monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
- **Change Control:** Branch protection with required approvals; documented change management including emergency procedures
- **Data Segregation:** Confirmed per-tenant data isolation (CC6.1.3); encryption at rest and in transit standard
- **Resilience Planning:** BCDR policy with annual testing across different loss scenarios; Multi-AZ deployment within Supabase

## Recommendation
**Investment Readiness: MODERATE**

Willow Voice demonstrates foundational security hygiene with a clean SOC 2 Type 1 opinion and strong access controls, but the architecture presents significant concentration risks. The extreme reliance on Supabase as a carve-out subservice organization combined with dependencies on niche AI providers (Baseten, Groq) creates a fragile supply chain with limited visibility into underlying infrastructure controls. 

The absence of disclosed RTO/RPO metrics, undefined backup frequencies, and lack of technical specificity regarding DevOps tooling suggest an immature operational posture inconsistent with enterprise-grade reliability requirements. 

**Recommended Conditions to Investment:**
1. Obtain Type 2 report (operating effectiveness over 6-12 months) 
2. Disclose and validate RTO/RPO metrics and backup frequencies
3. Document redundancy/failover plans for Baseten/Groq or migrate to tier-1 AI providers (AWS/Azure/GCP)
4. Implement multi-region disaster recovery
5. Add WAF and SIEM tooling; obtain cybersecurity insurance
6. Reduce Supabase dependency or obtain dedicated infrastructure audit rights

The company is suitable for early-stage investment with aggressive technical remediation requirements, but presents unacceptable risk for growth-stage or enterprise acquisition without substantial infrastructure hardening.
